name: Ida Plain
description: Iðavöllr (Ida Plain or Field) is a plain located in Asgard, the realm of the Æsir gods in Norse mythology. It holds immense significance as a central gathering place and a symbol of renewal and rebirth after Ragnarok, the twilight of the gods. Following the destruction of the old world, the surviving gods, including Baldr and Höðr who return from Hel, gather on Ida Plain. Here, they rebuild Asgard, reminisce about the past, and find the golden game pieces they had once used before the cataclysmic events. This act symbolizes the restoration of order, wisdom, and the continuation of divine presence. The plain is described as lush and fertile, signifying a new beginning and the promise of a prosperous future. Iðavöllr is also associated with craftsmanship and creation. It is mentioned that the dwarves, skilled artisans, built their forges and workshops on this plain, further emphasizing its importance as a center of activity and innovation. The resilience and continuity embodied by Iðavöllr offer a powerful message of hope and the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth within the Norse worldview. The plain represents not just a physical location, but also a spiritual and symbolic space where the gods reaffirm their commitment to the world and its future. Its association with peace, memory, and the rebuilding of civilization makes it a vital element in the Norse cosmological landscape.
subtitle: Plain of Rebirth and Gathering

keyMyths.title: Rebuilding After Ragnarok
keyMyths.description: After Ragnarok, the surviving gods gather on Ida Plain to rebuild Asgard and find the golden game pieces, symbolizing a new beginning.

keyMyths.title: The Golden Game Pieces
keyMyths.description: The discovery of the golden game pieces on Ida Plain reminds the gods of their past and reinforces their commitment to the future.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A key source for Norse mythology, including descriptions of Asgard and Ragnarok.
sources.text: A key source for Norse mythology, including descriptions of Asgard and Ragnarok.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems providing insights into Norse mythology, including Völuspá.
sources.text: A collection of Old Norse poems providing insights into Norse mythology, including Völuspá.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are paradisiacal plains associated with the afterlife or renewal. While Iðavöllr is a place of rebirth after destruction, Elysian Fields is a blessed realm for heroes.
Both are fertile, idealized locations symbolizing eternal life or a blessed existence after a cataclysmic event. Aaru is a paradise for the worthy dead, similar to Iðavöllr's role after Ragnarok.

Both represent a high plane of existence, a gathering place for elevated beings or those who have achieved spiritual merit. Brahmaloka is the abode of Brahma and a realm of purity, analogous to Iðavöllr's significance to the gods.

Both are idealized plains associated with renewal and a departure from the mortal world. Tír na nÓg is a land of eternal youth and beauty, offering a parallel to Iðavöllr's promise of rebirth and a new beginning.

associations.name: Rebirth
associations.significance: Ida Plain is where the surviving gods and new generations gather after Ragnarok, symbolizing a new beginning.

associations.name: Nine
associations.significance: Nine is associated with the nine realms connected by Yggdrasil, and Ida Plain's central location represents its connection to all of existence.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: The gods find the golden game pieces they used to play with on Ida Plain, representing a return to order and prosperity.

associations.name: Sunrise
associations.significance: As a place of renewal, Ida Plain is linked to the sunrise, symbolizing hope and the dawn of a new age after destruction.

associations.name: Grain
associations.significance: Grain represents fertility and abundance, reflecting Ida Plain as a place of growth and prosperity after Ragnarok.

associations.name: Assembly
associations.significance: Ida Plain is the central meeting place for the gods, signifying unity, council, and the establishment of order.

associations.name: Green
associations.significance: Green symbolizes growth and life, reflecting the rebirth and new beginnings associated with Ida Plain after Ragnarok.

companions.name: The Surviving Æsir Gods
companions.description: After Ragnarok, the surviving Æsir gods, such as Víðarr and Váli, gather on Ida Plain to rebuild and establish a new world. They are intrinsically linked to the plain's renewal.

companions.name: Móði and Magni
companions.description: The sons of Thor, Móði and Magni, inherit their father's hammer, Mjolnir, and are present on Ida Plain after Ragnarok, contributing to the new era. Their strength and inheritance tie them to the plain's future.

companions.name: Hoddmímis holt survivors
companions.description: Lif and Lifthrasir are two humans who survive Ragnarok by hiding in Hoddmímis holt (a grove or forest). They emerge to repopulate the earth, and thus are connected to Iðavöllr's role as a place of rebirth and new beginning.

companions.name: The Golden Tablets
companions.description: The golden tablets that the gods used for gaming in the past are found again on Ida Plain after Ragnarok. This symbolizes the return of wisdom and order.

extendedContent.title: Geography and Features of Iðavöllr
extendedContent.content: Iðavöllr, often translated as 'Ida Plain' or 'Ida Field,' is not described in great detail regarding its physical attributes in the surviving Norse texts. The Poetic Edda and Prose Edda offer glimpses, but do not provide a comprehensive map. It is understood to be a vast, open area within Asgard, the realm of the Æsir gods. Its significance lies less in its precise geography and more in its function as a central meeting point and symbolic space. While not explicitly stated, the presence of the gods suggests it is a fertile and pleasant place, suitable for their gatherings. The 'völlr' suffix implies a field or plain, conjuring an image of a broad, potentially grassy expanse. Some interpretations suggest it could be situated near or around the rebuilt halls of the gods, providing a communal space for their activities. It is also likely conceived as a place of order and stability in contrast to the chaos and destruction that precede and follow Ragnarok. The lack of detailed descriptions emphasizes its role as a symbolic landscape rather than a literal geographical location.

extendedContent.title: Sacred Significance and Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content: Iðavöllr's sacred significance is deeply interwoven with the concepts of cosmic order and renewal in Norse cosmology. Before Ragnarok, it serves as a meeting place for the Æsir, a council ground where they deliberate on matters of governance and destiny. This signifies its role in maintaining the existing order of the cosmos. More importantly, Iðavöllr is the site of rebirth and reconstruction after Ragnarok. The surviving gods, including Baldr and Höðr, returning from the realm of the dead, gather here to rebuild Asgard. This act represents the restoration of order from chaos, a new beginning for the cosmos. The discovery of the golden game pieces, which the gods used to play with in the past, further reinforces the theme of continuity and remembrance. This signifies not a complete break with the past, but a mindful rebuilding upon the foundations of what was lost. The re-establishment of divine rule and the construction of new, potentially even more magnificent, halls on Iðavöllr solidify its role as a symbol of hope and the cyclical nature of existence.

extendedContent.title: Events and Stories Associated with Iðavöllr
extendedContent.content: The most prominent event associated with Iðavöllr is the gathering of the surviving gods after Ragnarok. As described in the Prose Edda's *Gylfaginning*, Baldr and Höðr, once enemies, return from the realm of Hel and reconcile on this plain. This reunion symbolizes the overcoming of conflict and the establishment of harmony in the new world. They, along with Víðarr and Váli, sons of Odin, and Móði and Magni, sons of Thor, find the golden game pieces that the Æsir once used. This discovery sparks memories of the past and fuels their resolve to rebuild Asgard. While no specific battles or major dramatic events are explicitly said to have taken place *on* Iðavöllr before Ragnarok, its function as a gathering place for the gods implies that important decisions and pronouncements would have been made there. The lack of detailed narratives directly set on the plain enhances its symbolic importance, focusing attention on its role as a space for contemplation, reconciliation, and the restoration of cosmic order.

extendedContent.title: Modern Connections and Interpretations
extendedContent.content: Iðavöllr, though a relatively minor location in terms of named appearances, has garnered attention in modern interpretations of Norse mythology. Its symbolism of renewal and rebuilding resonates with contemporary anxieties and hopes. The post-Ragnarok scenario and the emergence of Iðavöllr as a place of new beginnings have been interpreted as allegories for societal resilience and the potential for positive change after periods of crisis. In neopagan and Heathen communities, Iðavöllr may be invoked as a symbol of hope and the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth. It can represent the ability to overcome challenges and rebuild from destruction, whether on a personal or communal level. Furthermore, the golden game pieces found on Iðavöllr serve as a poignant reminder of the past and the importance of preserving cultural memory while forging a new future. The lack of detailed description allows for diverse interpretations, making it a flexible and potent symbol in modern spiritual contexts.
